title: Labor slammed on NDIS cutbacks
full_text:
Labor is facing widespread condemnation from Paralympians, doctors and lawyers over its landmark NDIS reforms, which are being criticised for instituting co-payments by stealth, giving the government “Trump-like” powers and potentially forcing parents to medicate disabled children before being granted access to the scheme.
As the Coalition demands government release modelling underpinning its plans to slash annual NDIS growth from 11 per cent to less than 5 per cent in the next 18 months, a snap Senate inquiry into the proposed changes laid bare a stark lack of support for Labor’s bill in its current form.
Not a single witness who appeared before the Senate committee was supportive of the proposed changes, as of late Tuesday afternoon, with demands the government either scrap or drastically amend the reforms it hopes to pass in the next sitting fortnight.
The committee was warned the changes would leave some disabled Australians with less support, forcing them into hospitals and – in extreme cases – being at risk of fatal outcomes.
Villamanta Disability Rights Legal Service told the inquiry that actuaries had already forecast death rates to rise among severely disabled Australians as a result of the changes, while Paralympian Marayke Jonkers accused the government of treating disabled athletes like “performing monkeys”.
Ms Jonkers criticised the government for lauding disabled athletes on the world stage while cutting the $55bn-a-year NDIS responsible for ensuring people like her were not only able to participate in sport and other community activities, but also to “stay alive”.
“I can see a little … hypocrisy in the budget throwing money at Australia’s performance in the next Paralympic Games, while cutting the services that allow those Paralympians to actually stay alive,” she told the Senate committee, adding: “We are not performing monkeys.” Children and Young People with Disability Australia told the committee that the overhaul to eligibility – envisaged to ensure people sought all “appropriate treatment” before accessing the NDIS – presented significant dangers.
“Based on our reading of the bill and what we’ve been told by the Department of Health Disability and Ageing is that … the interpretation of appropriate treatment is quite broad and when I asked whether or not that would include medication the department advised ‘yes’,” she said.
“When we think about medication, we know that chemical restraints or a chemical restrictive practice can occur when somebody is forced to take medication as a way to suppress particular behaviours or conduct that either they wish to or people around them wish to.
“And that’s what we’re concerned about for children in particular, an early adoption of chemical restraints as a way of satisfying that need for appropriate treatment before non-medication support is available to them.” The Queenslanders with Disability Network also warned the bill risked breaching enshrined human rights allowing a person or family to refuse treatments they are uncomfortable with.
Labor senator and doctor Michelle Ananda-Rajah stressed the new eligibility thresholds would be carefully designed and informed by experts, describing the alarm over chemical restraints as a “narrative” that was not necessarily accurate.
Disability advocate Samantha Connor said she was most concerned by the minister of the day being given dramatic powers to reduce funding for certain supports and services as he or she saw fit, something other witnesses called a “Trump clause”.
“It is implicitly written into this bill that co-payments could be introduced,” she said. Debate has raged for years over whether to introduce a co-payment measure that would be means tested and ensure those that could pay for disability support did so, with Labor backbenchers even calling for the policy to be explored.
